summary: Iran's Hormuz closure reshapes global markets as T-MEC talks open in Mexico City today.
causalChain: Iran's shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz triggered the largest energy supply disruption in history, pushing global inflation higher and freezing central bank rate-cut plans; Mexico opens USMCA renegotiation talks TODAY against a backdrop of record exports, a just-ended Banxico easing cycle, and surging nearshoring FDI.

table.angle: Iran closes Hormuz — biggest oil shock in history, US gas at $4.55/gal
Fed frozen at 3.5–3.75%; dollar below 100, may need to hike
10-yr US borrowing cost falls to 4.59%; 15% emergency tariff hits metals
Banxico cuts to 6.50%, signals end of easing cycle
T-MEC talks open TODAY in Mexico City — $72B/month exports at stake
Mexico jumps to #19 in global FDI ranking on nearshoring wave
BoJ signals more rate hikes; yen at 159/USD, Iran war adds caution
PBOC hints at reserve cut; Hang Seng drops on China property woes
DAX +3.92% on Hormuz peace hopes; eurozone growth cut to 0.9%
ECB inflation forecast raised to 3%; rate-hike vs recession bind
BHP/Rio share Pilbara mines; 83% of output depends on China demand
RBA rate hikes + Iran energy shock raise Australia recession risk
Bitcoin funds bleed $1.26B in 6 days; BTC down 11% year-to-date
Digital-dollar supply hits $320B — 75% of all crypto trading uses them
SEC-CFTC classify BTC/ETH/SOL as commodities; MiCA enforcement starts July 1
Lab-grown food pipe works in living pigs — GOSH/UCL, Nature Biotechnology
87-year-old quantum prediction finally proved — opens new dark matter search, UCAS, Nature
Supercomputer solves how galaxies grow giant magnetic fields from plasma chaos, Nature

drafts.text: Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz — a narrow waterway where one-fifth of the world's oil travels — closed to ships in March 2026. The result: the biggest oil supply cut in recorded history. US gasoline jumped from $3.17 to $4.55 a gallon. Prices are climbing everywhere, and the US central bank — which was expected to lower borrowing costs this year — may now have to raise them instead to keep inflation in check.

The US central bank kept its borrowing rate steady at 3.5–3.75% this week. The dollar is below 100 on its main gauge — weak by recent norms — because investors fear the Iran war will keep prices high and slow the economy at the same time. Gold sits near $4,500 per ounce. When the dollar weakens, every dollar buys less oil and food on world markets, making inflation worse — a vicious cycle that rattles stocks and bonds.

What the US government pays to borrow money for 10 years just fell to 4.59% as investors bought safe bonds amid war fears. At the same time, the White House slapped a 15% emergency tax on certain imports. Steel, copper, and aluminum are getting more expensive as a result. US manufacturers warn their costs are rising — a squeeze that could slow hiring, raise consumer prices, and dampen the broader economy.

Mexico's central bank trimmed its main interest rate to 6.50% this week — the lowest since 2022 — and hinted it won't cut again soon. Lower rates make loans cheaper, helping businesses borrow and people buy homes. But the bank is cautious: prices in Mexico are still rising at 4.11% per year, above the 3% target. The peso trades near 19 per dollar, and the interest-rate gap between Mexico and the US still keeps capital flowing in.

TODAY, May 27, Mexico and the United States sit down in Mexico City to renegotiate the trade deal that covers nearly everything crossing the shared border. The formal deadline is July 1. Mexico's economy minister called the talks complex and difficult. What's at stake: Mexican exports to the US hit $72 billion last month, an all-time record. Canada is expected to join the three-way talks shortly after this first round ends.

Mexico jumped six spots to #19 in the global ranking of countries where businesses most want to invest. Companies are moving factories out of China and picking Mexico: labor costs $4.90/hour versus $6.10 in China, the USMCA trade deal eliminates most tariffs, and the US border is next door. Mexico pulled in a record $40.9 billion in foreign investment last year. The main drag: water shortages and power outages in key northern industrial corridors.

Japan's central bank said it plans to keep raising interest rates but is watching the Iran war carefully before acting. The Japanese yen sits at 159 per dollar — still weak by historical standards. A weak yen makes Japanese cars and electronics cheaper for overseas buyers, which boosts Toyota and Sony revenues. But it also makes imported oil and food more expensive for ordinary Japanese households, adding to a cost-of-living squeeze already under pressure.

China's central bank is hinting it will cut the amount of money banks must keep locked in reserve — releasing more cash into the economy. But markets aren't cheering: Hong Kong stocks fell because China's property sector is still deeply troubled. When developers can't pay debts, banks tighten lending, consumers feel poorer, and demand for steel and copper weakens. That slowdown ripples out to mines and factories across Asia and Australia.

Germany's main stock index jumped nearly 4% this week on hopes that a ceasefire could reopen oil flows through the Middle East. If ships move through the Strait again, European factories — which import almost all their energy — would see costs fall fast. But the EU already cut its growth forecast for 2026 to 0.9%, less than a cent gained per dollar of economic activity. The bounce is entirely conditional on the war winding down.

The European Commission raised its inflation forecast for the eurozone to 3% for 2026, up sharply from the 1.9% it predicted before the Iran war. Prices in Europe are rising faster than the European Central Bank's 2% target. The ECB faces a textbook bind: raise borrowing costs to cool inflation and risk pushing Germany — growing near zero — into recession, or hold steady and let prices run hot through the year.

The world's two biggest mining companies — BHP and Rio Tinto, both Australian — agreed to share neighboring iron ore mines in a remote corner of northwest Australia, targeting up to 200 million tonnes per year. The challenge: 83% of that iron ore goes to China, and Chinese demand is softening because fewer buildings are being built there. When China slows construction, Australian miners feel it almost immediately in their revenue.

Australia's central bank raised interest rates to fight inflation, but the combination of high borrowing costs and Iran-driven energy price spikes is hitting households and businesses at the same time. Some economists now warn Australia could tip into a technical recession — two straight quarters where the economy shrinks. Ironically, Australia exports natural gas, so the war-driven price spike brings extra export revenue while also raising costs at home.

For six trading days in a row, investors pulled more money out of US Bitcoin investment funds than they put in — about $1.26 billion withdrawn in one week. BlackRock and Fidelity's funds led the selling. Bitcoin is down 11% since January. With US prices rising at 3.8% a year, investors are fleeing riskier bets. When inflation fears spike, digital currencies — which pay no interest and produce no cash — tend to get sold first.

The total supply of "digital dollars" — coins always worth exactly $1, stored on computer networks — just hit $320 billion for the first time. Tether's version holds $189 billion; Circle's holds $77 billion. These digital dollars now power 75% of all crypto trading, acting like the cash register of the crypto world. When traditional banks won't touch crypto firms, traders use digital dollars to move value around the clock, across borders, without fees.

US market watchdogs made a landmark joint call: Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, and 13 other coins are officially commodities — like oil or gold — not stocks. That means the main stock regulator can no longer sue exchanges that list them for selling unregistered securities. Across the Atlantic, Europe's crypto rulebook enters full force on July 1: any company handling crypto for European customers must have a government license or shut down.

Scientists at Great Ormond Street Hospital and University College London grew a food pipe in the lab and transplanted it into a pig — and it worked. They stripped a donor organ of every cell, leaving a bare scaffold, then rebuilt it with the recipient pig's own cells. The body didn't reject it and no anti-rejection drugs were needed. This could one day help babies born without a working food pipe — about 1 in every 3,000 newborns. 📄 Published in Nature Biotechnology · More: https://www.ucl.ac.uk/news/2026/mar/engineered-tissue-offers-hope-babies-born-missing-food-pipe-section

For 87 years, physicists predicted that when an atom is hit hard, it should eject one of its own electrons — but no one could prove it. A team at the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences just filmed it happening for the first time, using custom-built gas detectors. The finding confirms a 1939 Soviet prediction. It also opens a new way to search for dark matter — the invisible substance thought to make up 85% of everything in the universe. 📄 Published in Nature · More: https://phys.org/news/2026-01-evidence-migdal-effect-path-dark.html

The universe is threaded with magnetic fields bigger than entire galaxies — and nobody knew how they got there. A supercomputer simulation published this week found the answer: inside the hot, swirling plasma that fills space, hidden steady flows act like giant generators, converting chaos into organized magnetic structures. The same physics that makes a power plant work operates at the scale of the cosmos. 📄 Published in Nature (May 26, 2026) · More: https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260525000503.htm
